edges.description: Failed central tolerance allows autoreactive T cells to escape and attack endocrine organs.
HLA-mediated susceptibility enables autoreactive B cell activation and autoantibody production.
Altered HLA peptide presentation promotes autoreactive T cell responses against endocrine antigens.
Adrenal cortex and steroid 21-hydroxylase autoimmunity can progress to autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency.
Thyroid-directed autoimmunity in APS can manifest as autoimmune thyroiditis.
Islet-directed autoimmunity can destroy pancreatic beta-cell function and produce type 1 diabetes mellitus.
APS-1-associated organ-specific autoimmunity can destroy parathyroid function and produce hypoparathyroidism.
APS-1 cytokine autoantibodies against IL-17/IL-22 pathway components can impair mucocutaneous antifungal defense.
Autoreactive cellular immunity against melanocytes can produce depigmented vitiligo lesions.
Autoreactive lymphocytic injury to gastric mucosa can manifest as autoimmune atrophic gastritis.
Autoreactive immune attack on hair-bearing tissue can produce alopecia in APS.
Autoimmune gonadal injury can reduce ovarian function and present as premature ovarian insufficiency.

nodes.description: In APS type 1, mutations in the AIRE gene impair thymic expression of tissue-restricted self-antigens in medullary thymic epithelial cells, leading to failure of negative selection and escape of autoreactive T cells into the periphery. AIRE controls the broad spectrum of self-antigens presented to developing thymocytes, and its deficiency results in multi-organ autoimmune destruction.

evidence.reference_title: AIRE deficiency, from preclinical models to human APECED disease.
evidence.snippet: Autoimmune polyendocrinopathy candidiasis ectodermal dystrophy (APECED) is a rare life-threatening autoimmune disease that attacks multiple organs and has its onset in childhood. It is an inherited condition caused by a variety of mutations in the autoimmune regulator (AIRE) gene that encodes a protein whose function has been uncovered by the generation and study of Aire-KO mice. These provided invaluable insights into the link between AIRE expression in medullary thymic epithelial cells (mTECs), and the broad spectrum of self-antigens that these cells express and present to the developing thymocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1: Clinical manifestations, pathogenetic features, and management approach.
evidence.snippet: APS-1 occurs because of mutations in the autoimmune regulator (AIRE) gene, leading to a disrupted mechanism of normal antigen expression, the formation of abnormal clones of immune cells, and autoimmune damage to various organs.

nodes.description: Autoimmune destruction of the adrenal cortex leading to primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease). Present in APS types 1, 2, and 4. Manifests as fatigue, hypotension, hyperpigmentation, and electrolyte abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune adrenal insufficiency and autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes: autoantibodies, autoantigens, and their applicability in diagnosis and disease prediction.
evidence.snippet: Autoimmune AD presented in four forms: as APS type 1 (13% of the patients), APS type 2 (41%), APS type 4 (5%), and isolated AD (41%).

nodes.description: Autoimmune hair loss, ranging from patchy alopecia to alopecia totalis. Non-endocrine component seen across APS types.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1: Clinical manifestations, pathogenetic features, and management approach.
evidence.snippet: APS-1 is often accompanied by hypogonadism, type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroiditis, vitiligo, alopecia, asplenia, pneumonitis, gastritis, pernicious anemia, and intestinal dysfunction, nephritis, and hepatitis.

nodes.description: Autoimmune gastritis with destruction of parietal cells, which may progress to pernicious anemia with vitamin B12 malabsorption. Seen across APS types.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1: Clinical manifestations, pathogenetic features, and management approach.
evidence.snippet: APS-1 is often accompanied by hypogonadism, type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroiditis, vitiligo, alopecia, asplenia, pneumonitis, gastritis, pernicious anemia, and intestinal dysfunction, nephritis, and hepatitis.

nodes.description: Organ-specific autoantibodies target endocrine gland antigens, contributing to gland destruction. Key autoantibodies include anti-21-hydroxylase (adrenal), anti-GAD65 and anti-IA2 (pancreatic islets), anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin (thyroid), and anti-IFN-omega and anti-IL-22 (APS-1 specific cytokine antibodies). These autoantibodies serve as diagnostic markers and may directly contribute to tissue damage.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune adrenal insufficiency and autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes: autoantibodies, autoantigens, and their applicability in diagnosis and disease prediction.
evidence.snippet: At the onset, all patients with autoimmune AD (100%) had detectable adrenal cortex and/or steroid 21-hydroxylase autoantibodies. In the course of natural history of autoimmune AD, the presence of adrenal cortex and/or steroid 21-hydroxylase autoantibodies identified patients at risk to develop AD.

Autoantibodies to the various endocrine and non-endocrine tissues not only offer a diagnostic clue to the autoimmune nature of diseases but also can be used to identify asymptomatic individuals who are at risk of developing other component diseases of the syndrome.

nodes.description: Persistent or recurrent candidal infections of skin, nails, and mucous membranes. Hallmark of APS type 1, typically the earliest manifestation. Related to autoantibodies against IL-17A, IL-17F, and IL-22.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1: Clinical manifestations, pathogenetic features, and management approach.
evidence.snippet: The classic triad of APS-1 includes chronic candidiasis of the skin and mucous membranes, adrenal insufficiency, and hypoparathyroidism.

nodes.description: In APS types 2-4, HLA class II susceptibility alleles (particularly HLA-DR3/DRB1*0301 and HLA-DR4/DRB1*0401) alter peptide presentation by antigen-presenting cells, predisposing to breakdown of peripheral tolerance against endocrine self-antigens. Additional susceptibility genes (CTLA4, PTPN22) further impair T cell regulation, but environmental triggers are also required since identical twins show incomplete concordance.

biological_processes: antigen processing and presentation via MHC class II

evidence.snippet: The nature of PAS has been based on the presence of lymphocyte infiltration in the affected gland, organ-specific antibodies in the serum, cellular immune defects and an association with the human leucocyte antigen (HLA) DR/DQ genes or immune response genes.
Genetic susceptibility is necessary but not sufficient to produce the disorder. This is illustrated by the lack of 100% concordance of disease in identical twins.
SPA type 2 presents with Addison's disease, type 1 diabetes, or autoimmune thyroid disease. Multiple genes have been implicated, including those of the class II major histocompatibility complex.

nodes.description: Autoimmune thyroiditis leading to hypothyroidism. Central feature of APS types 2 and 3. The most common autoimmune thyroid manifestation. Graves disease (hyperthyroidism) may also occur but is less frequent.

evidence.snippet: SPA type 3 is characterized by autoimmune thyroid disease and other autoimmune disease, excluding Addison's disease and hypoparathyroidism

nodes.description: Autoimmune destruction of parathyroid glands causing hypocalcemia. Most characteristic of APS type 1, where it is often the first endocrine manifestation.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1: Clinical manifestations, pathogenetic features, and management approach.
evidence.snippet: The classic triad of APS-1 includes chronic candidiasis of the skin and mucous membranes, adrenal insufficiency, and hypoparathyroidism.

nodes.description: Autoimmune-mediated gonadal failure with premature ovarian insufficiency, more common in females. Occurs in APS types 1 and 2.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1: Clinical manifestations, pathogenetic features, and management approach.
evidence.snippet: APS-1 is often accompanied by hypogonadism, type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroiditis, vitiligo, alopecia, asplenia, pneumonitis, gastritis, pernicious anemia, and intestinal dysfunction, nephritis, and hepatitis.

nodes.description: Autoreactive CD4+ and CD8+ T cells infiltrate endocrine glands, leading to progressive tissue destruction. CD8+ cytotoxic T cells directly kill endocrine cells targeting antigens such as 21-hydroxylase, while CD4+ T helper cells provide help for B cell autoantibody production and secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines. Regulatory T cell dysfunction further contributes to loss of peripheral tolerance.

evidence.reference_title: 21-Hydroxylase epitopes are targeted by CD8 T cells in autoimmune Addison's disease.
evidence.snippet: IFNγ ELISPOT responses against these peptides were stronger, broader and more prevalent among patients than in controls, whatever the disease presentation. Five peptides elicited T-cell responses in patients only (68% sensitivity, 100% specificity). Blocking experiments identified IFNγ-producing cells as CD8 T lymphocytes

evidence.reference_title: Transcriptional Changes in Regulatory T Cells From Patients With Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndrome Type 1 Suggest Functional Impairment of Lipid Metabolism and Gut Homing.
evidence.snippet: AIRE facilitates the expression of organ-specific transcripts in the thymus, which is essential for efficient removal of dangerous self-reacting T cells and for inducing regulatory T cells (Tregs). Although reduced numbers and function of Tregs have been reported in APS-I patients, the impact of AIRE deficiency on gene expression in these cells is unknown.

nodes.description: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells leading to insulin-dependent diabetes. Occurs in APS types 1, 2, and 3.

evidence.snippet: SPA type 2 presents with Addison's disease, type 1 diabetes, or autoimmune thyroid disease.

nodes.description: Autoimmune destruction of melanocytes causing depigmented skin patches. Common non-endocrine manifestation across all APS types.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 1: Clinical manifestations, pathogenetic features, and management approach.
evidence.snippet: APS-1 is often accompanied by hypogonadism, type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroiditis, vitiligo, alopecia, asplenia, pneumonitis, gastritis, pernicious anemia, and intestinal dysfunction, nephritis, and hepatitis.
